title: Tasks with cross-timezone due dates display wrong day in calendar view
body:
## Summary
When a user in Berlin (UTC+2) creates a task due at 11pm local, a viewer in San Francisco (UTC-7) sees it on the previous day in the calendar view, even though the underlying timestamp is correct.

## Reproduction
1. Set timezone to Europe/Berlin in user settings
2. Create task with due date 2026-04-22 23:00
3. Switch timezone to America/Los_Angeles
4. Open calendar view → task appears on 2026-04-21

## Expected
Task should appear on the day matching the *creator's intended date*, not the viewer's local conversion. Per product spec, due dates are 'day-anchored' not 'instant-anchored'.

## Notes
This bites enterprise customers with global teams. Acme Corp flagged it during last week's check-in.

comments.body: Looked at this. The issue is in `formatDueDate.ts` — we store due dates as ISO instants but the calendar grid bucketizes by viewer-local day. Need to introduce a `dueDateMode` field per workspace: 'instant' or 'day-anchored', defaulting to 'day-anchored' for new workspaces.

Agree on day-anchored as default. From a UX perspective most users think of 'due Friday' not 'due 11:59pm Friday in some timezone'. We'll need migration messaging for existing customers since this is a behavior change — I can draft an in-product banner.
Tagging Jordan — this might be worth a small RFC since it affects the public API contract on `/api/tasks` (we'd be adding a new field to responses).

title: Slack integration: send task notifications to channels and DMs
body:
## Customer ask
Multiple customers (most recent: Acme Corp, Northwind, Helios) have asked for Slack notifications when:
- A task is assigned to them
- A task they own changes status
- A task they're watching is commented on

## Scope (v1)
- OAuth Slack app, per-workspace install
- Per-user notification preferences (channel, DM, off)
- Map Loopline users to Slack users via email
- Notification templates: assigned, status_changed, commented, mentioned

## Out of scope (v1)
- Slack → Loopline (creating tasks from Slack)
- Custom notification templates per workspace
- Multi-Slack-workspace mapped to single Loopline workspace

## Open questions
- Throttling: Acme has projects with 200+ daily updates. Bundle? Rate-limit per recipient?
- How do we handle users who installed the app but haven't mapped their Slack identity?

comments.body: From sales: this is the #1 ask in deals over $1K MRR. Helios specifically said it's a blocker for their renewal. Cannot stress this enough.
Scoped this with Sam and Lisa. Targeting v1 in May. Will use the Slack Bolt SDK. We'll throttle to a max of 1 notification per task per user per 5 min — Acme's volume would be unusable otherwise.

Started scoping the implementation. Will use the same single-transaction batched-insert pattern from PR #863 — learned the hard way from the 4/14 incident not to do per-recipient connections in fanout paths.

title: Migrate API from Express 4 to Fastify
body:
## Why
- Express 4 is in maintenance-only mode; Express 5 has been stuck in beta for years
- Fastify is ~2x faster on our workload (benched p95 on /api/tasks)
- Better TypeScript ergonomics (built-in schema validation)
- Native async/await throughout — we have ~30 places where Express middleware quirks bite us

## Why not
- ~3 weeks of engineering time
- Risk: 100+ existing routes to port
- We have working software

## Plan (when we do it)
Incremental: stand up Fastify alongside Express, route by route via reverse proxy. Migrate hottest routes first. Decommission Express when migration is complete.

comments.body: I want to do this but it's hard to justify against feature work. Re-evaluating each quarter. Q2 plan does not include this; Q3 might.
Discussed in eng leads sync. Deferring to Q3 at earliest. Not blocking anything currently. Will revisit at Q3 planning; closing if no movement by then.

title: Connection pool exhaustion under high notification fanout load
body:
## Incident
2026-04-14, 14:23–14:35 UTC. ~12 minutes of api-prod 503s. Triggered by deploy of PR #847 (in-app task notifications fanout).

## Root cause
`fanoutNotifications` opened a DB connection per recipient inside `Promise.all`. The error path did not call `connection.release()`. With 500+ recipients on the Acme workspace, every Promise.all rejection leaked connections until the pool (max 100) was empty.

## Fix
PR #863: refactored fanout to use a single transaction with batched inserts. Removed per-recipient connection acquisition. Added `try/finally` for any connection acquired outside transaction context.

## Postmortem
https://loopline.notion.site/postmortem-2026-04-14

comments.body: Detected via Datadog p95 latency monitor. Mitigated by rolling back to v4.12.3. Total downtime ~12 min. Customer impact: Acme Corp during their NA launch event; two smaller customers also affected per support tickets.

PR #863 up with the fix. Added an integration test that simulates a 1000-recipient fanout to verify the pool stays healthy. Also added a `RDS connection usage > 80%` Datadog monitor as a preventive measure.
Postmortem complete and reviewed in eng all-hands. All three action items (Datadog alert, lint rule, postmortem doc) are done. Closing.

title: Drag-and-drop reordering for nested subtasks (>2 levels deep)
body:
## Problem
Current drag-and-drop only works at the top-level task list. Users with nested subtasks (we support up to 4 levels) have to manually edit task order in the side panel.

## User feedback
- 14 support tickets in March mentioning this
- Acme uses 4-level nesting heavily for their project breakdowns
- Helios opened this as a blocker for adoption with their 50-person eng team

## Design
Nora has mocks: see linked Figma. Key UX decisions:
1. Drag handle visible on hover only (avoid clutter)
2. Drop zones distinguish (a) reorder among siblings vs (b) reparent to a new parent — visually distinct
3. Constraint: cannot reparent a node into its own descendant (loop prevention) — show invalid drop visually

## Technical notes
We use a materialized-path approach (`path` column, e.g. `1/4/12/3`). Reordering siblings is cheap; reparenting requires updating descendants' paths in a transactional update that can be slow on tasks with many children. Need to benchmark against prod-sized data.

comments.body: Figma here: figma.com/loopline/dnd-subtasks. Also did a usability test with 5 users on the prototype — 4/5 figured out reparenting without prompting. The 5th was confused by the drop-zone color, so I'm changing it from blue to green for reparent vs. reorder to disambiguate.

Bench result: reparenting a node with 5K descendants takes ~1.2s on prod-sized DB. We should debounce the API call and show optimistic UI client-side. Will write up the approach in a design doc before starting implementation.
